column1: All overall, child, race/ethnicty food insecurity estiamtes produced by Feeding America as part of the Map the Meal Gap project are modeled estimates and should be interpreted as approximations (e.g., a community with an estimated food insecurity rate of 0% may still contain individuals and/or households that experience food insecurity).
Blank cells indicate that values are currently unavailable, not applicable and/or data required to produce local estimates were unavailable.
Notes on Localized Meal Costs and Food Budget Shortfalls

Due to a change in methodology, local meal cost, food budget shortfall, and meal gap estimates for 2023 are not directly comparable to estimates from previous years. Please reference the technical appendix on our website for more details regarding this change and contact research@feedingamerica.org for guidance on comparisons over time.
Notes on Estimates of Food Insecurity by Race and Ethnicity

Estimates by race/ethnicity are limited to subpopulations for which there are adequate public data that follow Feeding America’s data release rules. Many estimates by race/ethnicity have wider confidence intervals than estimates for the overall population due to smaller sample sizes; as a result, the dataset excludes estimated numbers of people experiencing food insecurity by race/ethnicity and includes only rounded rates, helping to avoid conveying a false sense of precision and to encourage users of the data to focus on relative disparities, both within and across geographies. These estimates are directional and should be considered in conjunction with other local data as well as insights from the individuals who live in these communities.

For more information about what we know about food insecurity among these groups or for additional information about study inputs and outputs related to race and ethnicity our website (see link at right) and/or the public landing page at map.feedingamerica.org.
Visit our website (linked at right) for more information about food insecurity estimates for seniors and older adults
Notes on Estimated Income Eligibility among the Food Insecure Population

Historically, the Map the Meal Gap dataset included the estimated percentage of food insecure individuals in households with incomes a) at or below their state's gross income limit for SNAP (i.e., 130%, 160%, 165%, 185% or 200% of the Federal Poverty Level); b) above the federal gross income limit for other federal nutrition programs such as WIC (i.e., 185% of FPL); and c) between these two income thresholds (e.g., between 130% and 185% of FPL). Beginning in 2023 (2021 data), however, the dataset no longer includes these latter income eligibility estimates for the middle thresholds since it was determined that changes in these estimates were more attributable to the methodology used to derive estimates for the lower and upper thresholds than to actual changes in household and/or community characteristics. Because the most recent income eligibility estimates for the overall food insecure population found in this dataset now focus exclusively on SNAP eligibility, the MMG 2024 dataset now includes three new columns that clarify the estimated share of food insecure individuals living with incomes above and below the SNAP threshold for each state. Each of the columns here are defined below under Data Definitions.
Notes on Estimates for Seniors and Older Adults

This dataset now contains state-level and metro-level food insecurity estimates for seniors and older adults. Note that as of the 2024 release (2022 data) is not a continuation of The State of Senior Hunger report series. Therefore, due to changes in methodology/approach for the 2024 and 2025 releases, we advise caution in making (specific) comparisons to previous releases of senior and older adult food insecurity data.

The Census Bureau officially adopted Connecticut planning regions as county equivalents with the 2022 data releases. Since the geographic boundaries of the planning regions and counties do not align, local estimates of food insecurity and other measures for geographies within Connecticut for 2022 and 2023 are even less directly comparable to estimates from previous years than they otherwise would be.

column2: County estimates are estimated using Feeding America's Map the Meal Gap model
District estimates are estimated using Feeding America's Map the Meal Gap model

State food insecurity estimates reflect aggregated district estimates, with the exception of estimates for seniors (60+) and older adults (50-59) which are direct estimates from the Current Population Survey.

Metro area estimates are for senior and older adults in metropolitan areas with at least 1,000,000 people, and estimates for 2019-2021 are from the State of Senior Hunger and Hunger Among Older Adults reports. Estimates for all years are direct estimates from the Current Population Survey.

National-level estimates of food insecurity across selected demographic and socioeconomic categories for both seniors (60+) and older adults (50-59). These are estimated from the Current Population Survey.
